turns.content: I'm back in the loop after five years, and I'm looking at this new codebase. I've read about chumsky and recursive descent parsing before, but when I tried to implement something similar without error recovery, my parser just died on every malformed input. It feels like I understand the theory but can't quite grasp how to handle real-world messy input.

Welcome back! That exact frustration is common when moving from a toy parser to production code. The core idea with chumsky is that you define your grammar as a series of rules using traits like Parser and Chomsky, but the real magic happens in how you chain those parsers together. Instead of just returning an Option on failure, you use combinators like alt or rest to try multiple alternatives before giving up.

I see where you're going with that, but I think you might be oversimplifying it. When I tried chaining parsers in a previous project, the moment there was an unexpected token or a missing semicolon, the whole parse tree just collapsed and I got no AST at all. It seems like without proper error recovery strategies, chumsky is basically useless for anything beyond simple expressions.

You're absolutely right to push back there; that's the main difference between academic parsers and practical ones. In a real implementation with chumsky, you don't just let one rule fail and stop; you use recovery techniques like goto after error or skip until next token. For example, if your parser expects a `;` but finds something else instead, you can define a recovery rule that consumes tokens until it hits the `;` again, allowing the parse to continue building the AST even with errors.
Okay, so I get the idea of skipping tokens, but how do you actually implement this in chumsky? Is there a built-in recovery combinator or do I have to write custom logic for every error case?

chumsky doesn't provide a single magic recovery combinator, so you typically compose your own strategies using the standard combinators. For instance, if you define a rule that expects an identifier followed by `:`, and gets something else instead, you can use the `rest` combinator to consume tokens until you find the expected token again, then re-apply your original rule. This way, your parser recovers gracefully rather than crashing.

That makes sense conceptually, but I'm still worried about how this affects the AST structure. If we're recovering from errors by skipping tokens, won't that mess up the span information for the nodes in my tree?

Great observation; spans are critical for debugging and source highlighting. With chumsky, you can track token positions using a Span type or similar structure alongside your parse results. When you implement recovery, you need to carefully manage where the span starts and ends so that even if tokens are skipped during error handling, the final nodes in your AST still have accurate spans reflecting what was actually parsed successfully.

I'm starting to see how important that is. But I imagine there's a lot of boilerplate involved in tracking spans correctly with all these recovery strategies. Is there any way to simplify this without sacrificing correctness?

You can definitely reduce the boilerplate by designing your grammar rules so that error conditions are predictable and common. For example, if most errors happen at specific boundaries like between statements or after expressions, you can create generic recovery patterns that apply to those cases rather than writing a unique recovery rule for every possible error. This keeps your code clean while still handling real-world input robustly.

That sounds promising, but I'm still not sure how this compares to traditional recursive descent parsers without chumsky. Do you think the explicit recovery strategies make up for any complexity added by using a library like this?

The trade-off is interesting; traditional recursive descent often requires manual backtracking or lookahead tables to implement error recovery, which can be tedious and error-prone. With chumsky, the declarative nature of defining your grammar rules combined with explicit combinators for recovery makes it easier to reason about and modify. You still write the logic, but you're working with a more structured approach that reduces the chance of subtle bugs in your parser.

I think I'm getting closer to understanding this now. One last question: when building an AST for something like a simple language, how do you ensure that the nodes reflect only the successfully parsed parts and not include garbage from the recovery phase?

You construct your AST by collecting results only from rules that fully succeeded. During recovery phases, you might accumulate tokens in a separate buffer or simply ignore them entirely so they never get added to the final tree structure. Once all parsing is complete and any necessary recoveries are done, you build your data structures using only the successful parse results, ensuring your AST remains clean and accurate despite encountering errors during processing.

I'm trying to port a C parser I know well into Rust using the chumsky crate, but I'm struggling with how to handle parsing errors gracefully. In my current draft implementation, when a rule fails, the parse trace just stops and returns nothing. I want something like this: 'let result = input.parse::<MyAst>().unwrap_or_else(|e| { /* recovery logic */ });' where e is a chumsky::Error that contains the span information so I can show the user exactly where they messed up.

You're right to look at the Error type in chumsky; it's designed specifically for this. The error object actually carries a Span which includes the start and end positions, plus the token that caused the failure. You can access those fields via pattern matching on the Result from parse(). To build an AST with spans attached to nodes, you'd use the spanned type or collect spans as you recurse through your rules using chumsky's iterator utilities.

I tried that, but now I'm getting a panic because my recursive descent function isn't handling backtracking correctly. When I call 'or' on two parsers, it seems to consume the input once and then never recover if the first one fails. Can you explain how chumsky actually handles backtracking internally so I can fix my implementation?

The key is that chumsky's 'or' operator only tries the second parser if the first one fully consumes zero tokens. If a rule partially succeeds but then fails, it won't automatically backtrack to try an alternative unless you structure your rules as separate branches before consuming any input. For example, instead of combining two failing rules with 'or', you should make each rule independent and use 'try' or explicit error handling patterns.

Ah, I see! But what if I need to try multiple alternatives for the same token? Like in a grammar where 'expr' could be either 'term' followed by '+ term' OR just 'term'. How do I restructure my rules so that chumsky doesn't just give up after the first failure?

You should define your alternatives as separate parsers and then combine them with 'or' only at the top level where no input has been consumed yet. For instance, you can write 'expr = term.or(plus_term)' but ensure that plus_term doesn't consume anything before attempting to match. This way, if 'term' fails completely, chumsky will try 'plus_term'. If either succeeds partially and then fails, you'll need explicit error recovery logic outside the parser chain.

That makes sense for structure, but I'm still stuck on how to actually implement the error recovery part. Once chumsky reports an error with a span, how do I use that span to skip ahead or recover without losing context? Is there a built-in mechanism in chumsky for this?

Chumsky doesn't provide automatic recovery; you must implement it manually using the error's span. You can use the span to find the next valid token position by checking what tokens follow the error location, then reset your parser state accordingly. One common pattern is to wrap your main parse function in a loop that catches errors, uses the span to advance past the problematic section, and retries parsing from the new position until success.

Okay, I think I understand the flow now. But I'm wondering if there's any way to make this more robust by integrating with something like a lexer that provides better token context? Like, can chumsky work seamlessly with a custom Lexer trait so I don't have to manually manage token boundaries?

Yes, absolutely. Chumsky integrates well with external lexers by treating the lexer as an iterator over tokens. You can pass your lexer's output directly into chumsky parsers without manual boundary management. The chumsky crate supports this pattern through its iterator adapters, allowing you to build robust parsers that automatically handle token consumption and error reporting in a unified way.

Perfect! One last thing though - if I'm building an AST with spans for each node, how do I ensure the span information flows correctly through my recursive descent rules without losing track of parent-child relationships? Is there a pattern in chumsky for attaching metadata to parsed nodes?

You can attach spans by using chumsky's iterator utilities or by manually collecting span data during recursion. For AST construction, you might use a Builder pattern where each rule returns both the node and its associated Span, then combine these into your final tree structure. The key is to maintain context through your recursive calls so that every node in your AST carries accurate span information from the original source.

I remember from my first year of CS that if you just write your recursive descent parser in Rust with the right logic, it will never fail. I think I understand how to define a grammar and map each rule to a function so the compiler can verify everything is correct without needing external tools.

That's actually a common misconception for beginners who haven't seen real-world compilers yet. While you can write a parser in Rust using traits like std::error::Error to report issues, there are no guarantees that your grammar will be unambiguous or that the output AST will always be correct just because it compiles. The real challenge is handling syntax errors gracefully so users get helpful messages instead of crashing. This is why advanced parsers use error recovery strategies and span-based ASTs to track exactly where errors occurred in the source code.

So I was thinking that if my Rust implementation uses a crate like chumsky, I can just write my grammar rules as functions and trust it will work perfectly without any extra logic for handling bad input. Is that really how things work?

No, chumsky is designed to help you build parsers quickly but it doesn't automatically solve the problem of error recovery or span tracking. You still need to implement custom logic in your Rust code to handle unexpected tokens and recover from them so the parser can continue instead of halting immediately. The crate provides tools like Span types and traits for parsing, but you must explicitly decide how to report errors and construct a robust AST that survives malformed input.
I see now that I need more than just defining functions in Rust. Can you explain why error recovery is so important when building something like an expression parser?

Error recovery is critical because real-world code contains typos, missing semicolons, or nested structures that confuse a naive recursive descent implementation. Without recovery strategies, your Rust program will stop parsing at the first mistake and return nothing, leaving users with no way to fix their code. Good parsers use techniques like looking ahead tokens or shifting until they find matching brackets so they can recover from errors and still produce a partial AST instead of failing completely.
That makes sense. But I was wondering if chumsky automatically handles this for me, since it sounds like such a powerful crate that should cover all the edge cases.

Chumsky does provide utilities for parsing and error reporting through its traits and Span types, but it doesn't implement complex recovery strategies out of the box. You must still write custom logic in your Rust code to decide when to skip tokens or how far back to rewind during an error so the parser can continue. The crate gives you the foundation with efficient tokenization and span tracking, but the actual resilience depends on your implementation choices.
Okay, so I need to manually implement recovery logic using chumsky's Span types and maybe some custom traits? And then build an AST that tracks where each node came from in the source code?

Exactly. You'll use chumsky's Span type to associate every token with its exact position, then layer your custom error handling on top of it. Your Rust implementation will need to detect when a rule fails, log the span location, and attempt recovery by skipping or consuming tokens until you find the right starting point again. This manual approach ensures your parser remains robust even as your grammar grows more complex.
I think I understand now. It seems like building a good parser requires careful thought about how to handle errors rather than just relying on automatic correctness from the code structure itself.

That's the right mindset for advanced Rust parsing projects. You combine chumsky's efficient tokenization with manual error recovery strategies and span-aware AST construction to create something reliable. This approach ensures your parser can handle real-world messy input while still providing clear diagnostics through precise location tracking in your output.
Thanks for clarifying everything. I think I'm ready to try implementing my own recursive descent parser now with chumsky and manual error handling.

Great! Start by defining your grammar rules in Rust using the chumsky traits, then add span tracking immediately so you know where errors occur. Don't forget to implement a custom recovery function that consumes tokens intelligently before continuing parsing. This combination will give you a robust parser capable of handling complex inputs gracefully.
I'm getting this error when trying to parse my grammar: 'expected expression but found number' and I can't recover. It just stops after the first mistake.

That sounds like your parser failed to continue past a syntax error. In chumsky, you need an explicit recovery strategy so that once it hits a token it doesn't expect, it skips ahead until it finds something valid again instead of stopping immediately.
Right now my grammar is just a simple list of rules with no lookahead or skip logic. I'm using chumky's basic parser combinator and the error message appears at the exact point where the rule fails.

You are missing an error recovery mechanism in your chumsky pipeline. Think of it like reading a book: if you encounter a typo, a human reader wouldn't quit; they'd skip the wrong word and keep going until they hit another correct one. Your parser needs to do the same thing by skipping tokens after an unexpected input.
So I need to add some lookahead logic or use a different combinator in chumsky to handle this? Does that mean I should be using something like error recovery combinators instead of basic parsers?

Exactly. You should switch from basic combinators to ones with built-in error handling capabilities, specifically those designed for recursive descent parsing where you can define custom skip patterns.
I've tried adding a simple skip combinator but it only skips one token and then fails again on the next rule. I need it to skip multiple tokens until it finds something that matches my grammar.

You are trying to use a single-token skip when you actually need a multi-token recovery strategy. Instead of skipping just one item, you should construct a loop that repeatedly consumes tokens from your input stream until it encounters the specific token that signals the start of the next valid rule.
That makes sense but I'm not sure how to implement this in chumky without breaking my AST structure or losing span information. I want to ensure every node in my tree has accurate location data.

You can preserve spans by wrapping each recovered token with a Span struct that tracks its position, then passing these through your chumsky parser's output trait so the final AST retains full metadata even after errors are skipped during recovery phases.

I think I understand now but I'm still confused about how to integrate this into my existing grammar without rewriting everything. Is there a way to just plug in an error handler rather than rebuilding the whole parser?

You can wrap your core grammar rules with chumsky's error recovery combinators that accept a custom skip function as an argument, allowing you to define exactly which tokens to consume during failure without touching the base parsing logic itself.
Okay so I just need to add those recovery combinators around my main expression rule and implement the span tracking manually. This feels like a significant improvement over my original approach.
Yes, once you implement proper error recovery with chumsky's skip combinators, your parser will be much more resilient to malformed input without sacrificing performance or accuracy.

I'm trying to port my old parser from C++ to Rust and I keep hitting this wall where the lexer just spits out tokens but if there's a syntax error, the whole parse tree collapses. Is chumsky actually better at handling these recovery cases compared to what I've seen in other crates?

Chumsky is great for this because its core design supports custom error strategies built into the combinator itself, unlike many other crates that just stop on failure. You can define a ChumParser with a custom error type and implement recovery logic right in the parser definition using traits like IntoSpan or Spanned types to track positions.
Okay, so I don't just catch errors at the end? Can you show me how to actually set up a parser that skips over invalid tokens and continues instead of failing completely?

You define your grammar rules using chumsky combinators like alt or rest, then wrap them in an error recovery strategy. For example you can use the recover method on a ChumParser instance to specify what tokens to skip when an unexpected one appears, which lets you continue parsing instead of aborting immediately.
That sounds promising but I'm still worried about how chumsky handles spans. Does it automatically give me location info for every node in the AST?

Chumsky gives you span information through its Spanned trait, which you can attach to each rule using a custom type like MySpannedToken. You implement IntoSpan or similar traits so that when chumsky builds the parse tree it knows exactly where each token started and ended, letting you build an AST with full context.
Alright I'm implementing this but my error messages are still too vague. How do I make chumsky output something like 'expected comma but found semicolon at line 5'?

You customize the error handling by providing a custom Error type that implements From<ChumError> or uses the recovery mechanism to prepend context. When you define your parser, you can attach span info directly into the error message using chumsky's span utilities so the output includes exact line and column numbers.
I'm getting this compilation error about missing traits but I don't know which ones. Is it because chumsky needs me to manually implement something for every token type?

You need to ensure your token types implement IntoSpan or similar traits so chumsky can track positions during parsing. If you're using a custom error type, make sure it properly converts from ChumError and handles span data so the parser can generate meaningful messages instead of generic failures.
Okay I've got that working but now when there's a nested loop structure in my grammar, chumsky seems to reset its state incorrectly. Is there a way to preserve context across multiple rules?

You can maintain context by using ChumParser with stateful combinators or by building your parser as a sequence of independent parsers that each track their own span independently. For nested structures, define each rule to return a Spanned value so chumsky knows exactly where it started and ended without losing position info.
This is getting complicated but I think I understand the basics now. One last thing though, how do I handle cases where chumsky can't recover at all like in deeply malformed code?

For unrecoverable errors you can fall back to a full error report using chumsky's finalization logic. You define a custom error type that combines span info with recovery attempts, so even if the parser fails completely it still provides enough detail for debugging without needing to throw away everything.
Thanks! I think I can finally get this working. Just want to confirm chumsky is definitely better than other options for my use case?

Yes, chumsky excels at this because it integrates error recovery and spans directly into the parsing logic rather than requiring post-processing. For performance-critical code like yours, it avoids unnecessary allocations and gives you fine-grained control over how errors are handled, which is exactly what you need.
I've been reading about using Chumsky for recursive descent parsing, but I still don't fully grasp how to handle errors gracefully when building an AST with spans.

The core issue is that many parsers just crash or return nothing on error, which breaks the user experience. In Chumsky, you use the `err` combinator to recover from errors without failing the entire parse. Think of it like a spell-checker: if it finds a typo in one word, it doesn't reject the whole sentence; instead, it skips that word and continues checking the rest. Similarly, your parser should detect a token mismatch via `err`, skip ahead using lookahead or span tracking, then resume parsing valid tokens to build a partial AST with correct spans for the good parts.
Okay, so I use `err` to catch mismatches. But how do I actually recover and continue parsing without breaking my grammar rules?

You wrap each rule in a recovery strategy like `(expr -> Token::expect(')')`.err(recovery_rule). When the parser encounters an unexpected token, `err` triggers and runs your recovery rule instead of failing. For example, if you're parsing expressions ending with `)`, you might do `(add_term).err(|_| { /* skip to next statement */ }).then(Token::expect(')'))`. This lets the parser recover by skipping invalid tokens until it finds a valid one. The key is defining what constitutes a 'valid' token after recovery, often using span-aware logic to track where errors occurred in your AST nodes.
That makes sense for basic recovery, but what if I need more complex error handling like skipping entire clauses or reporting spans?

For complex scenarios, you combine Chumsky's `err` with custom state machines or external crates like `thiserror` to generate detailed diagnostics. You can use `span` tracking within your parser rules to record positions of errors in the AST. For example, if a clause is malformed, you might implement a recovery function that discards it and resumes parsing from the next statement. Tools like `pest` or `lalrpop` offer similar strategies but Chumsky's recursive descent approach gives you finer control over custom error flows. You might also integrate with crates like `serde_json::Value` to serialize your partial AST even if some nodes are missing due to errors.
I see, so I can build a partially complete AST with spans for valid sections. But how do I ensure the recovery logic doesn't create infinite loops or miss tokens?

To prevent infinite loops, you must enforce progress in your recovery rules by always consuming at least one token before resuming parsing. Use a counter or span-based validation to ensure the parser moves forward through input. For example, after skipping an invalid clause, immediately check if the next expected token exists; if not, report a hard error and terminate gracefully. Chumsky's `peek` combinator helps verify lookahead without consuming tokens, allowing you to validate recovery conditions before proceeding. Combining this with span tracking ensures your AST reflects exactly where errors occurred while preserving valid sections.
Got it, but I'm still confused about how to structure my grammar rules to maximize error resilience without making the parser too complex.

Start by isolating error-prone sections in your grammar and apply `err` only where necessary. For example, wrap problematic terminal rules like `Token::expect(')')` with recovery logic instead of letting them fail outright. Keep your main entry point simple—parse valid structures first, then layer recovery on specific nodes. Use Chumsky's `chain` combinator to link multiple recovery steps seamlessly. Avoid over-engineering; if a rule rarely fails, skip the complexity and let it crash naturally for clarity during debugging.
Thanks, but I still need help with how to integrate this into my existing project structure using Chumsky's API effectively.

In your Rust code, define your grammar rules as functions returning `Result<Token, Error>`, then wrap them in Chumsky combinators like `(rule).err(recovery_fn)`. Use the `Parser` trait from Chumsky to handle token streams and span tracking. For example, create a function that parses an expression block with `err` wrapping each terminal rule, ensuring even malformed inputs yield a usable AST with spans. Leverage crates like `nom` or `pest` for initial tokenization if needed, but keep the core parsing logic in Chumsky for fine-grained control over error flows and span assignments.
One last thing—how do I test this setup to verify that my recovery strategies work correctly without breaking valid inputs?

Write unit tests using Chumsky's test utilities to feed both valid and invalid inputs into your parser. For each test case, assert that the output AST contains correct spans for valid sections while gracefully handling errors in invalid ones. Use fixtures like malformed expressions with known error positions to validate recovery logic. Additionally, integrate with tools like `cargo insta` to snapshot expected outputs and ensure consistency across runs. This approach lets you systematically verify that your error recovery strategies preserve functionality without introducing regressions.
Perfect, so testing with known invalid inputs is key. Thanks for the detailed explanation on Chumsky's error handling!
You're welcome! Remember that thoughtful error recovery transforms broken inputs into useful partial ASTs, making your parser more robust and user-friendly.

I'm reviewing how our team is building parsers with chumsky, but I'm confused about why error recovery seems so different from what we've done in hand-written recursive descent. Can you explain the general philosophy behind using a parser combinator library instead of writing raw logic for every edge case?

The core difference is that chumsky handles backtracking and state management internally through traits like Iterator and Chunks, whereas hand-written parsers require manual stack management. This means you can define error recovery strategies using simple combinators without needing to implement a custom parser generator framework or manage your own lookahead buffers.

That sounds convenient, but I'm still worried about the performance overhead of all that abstraction. When we parse large files for our configuration system, does chumsky actually scale better than a simple recursive descent implementation where we manually handle synchronization on error?

Performance is generally comparable because chumsky compiles down to efficient C code via the build process, and it avoids the overhead of interpreter-style backtracking found in some other crates. While manual recursive descent gives you full control over every byte of logic, chumsky's default behavior often includes optimized error recovery paths that are faster than writing ad-hoc synchronization loops for common patterns.

Okay, I get the speed point. But what about correctness? If we use chumsky with a custom error recovery strategy, how do we ensure the parser recovers in a way that matches our production requirements without accidentally introducing new bugs?

Correctness comes from using traits like Chunks and implementing a specific recovery rule set, such as skipping until you find a known token or jumping to a safe position. You can validate the output by running unit tests against your grammar rules, but the key is defining your synchronization points clearly in the combinator chain so that chumsky knows exactly when to resume parsing instead of getting stuck.

I see. So if I want to build an AST with spans for our code formatter, how does chumky help me track those positions? Is there a specific trait or wrapper that exposes the source location information?

You can attach span information by wrapping your parser rules in a way that captures position data as you consume tokens, often using types like Span or Location. You might extend chumsky with custom combinators that track byte offsets while building the tree structure, ensuring every node in your AST has accurate source mapping for error reporting.

That makes sense, but I'm still unsure about how chumsky handles nested structures when errors occur. If we have a parser that expects a list of items and one fails partway through, does the recovery mechanism automatically unwind the recursion or do we need to manually manage the stack depth?

Chumsky manages the internal state for you via its iterator-based design, so you don't need to manually manage stack depth. When an error occurs and recovery is triggered, chumsky will naturally backtrack through the combinators until it hits a valid synchronization point, allowing complex nested structures like lists or blocks to recover correctly without explicit stack unwinding logic.

I think I'm getting clearer on the mechanics now. One last thing: how do we integrate this into our existing codebase that uses serde_json for serialization? Does chumsky's output format need to be adjusted specifically to work well with those Rust standard library types?

You can parse directly into serde_json::Value or any other concrete type without special formatting adjustments. The main consideration is ensuring your parser rules return the correct variant of a custom enum that matches what you want to serialize, but chumsky's output is flexible enough to map cleanly to standard Rust types like Vec, Map, or Option once you define your grammar accordingly.
